title: Harness Lab
subtitle: Workshop operating system for working with AI agents
principles: Work so a fresh reader can continue

phases.label: Opening and orientation
phases.goal: Open the day as a shared start: why harness engineering matters right now, what the room will do from the first beat to the reveal, and how we will know the work can stand on its own when a fresh reader picks it up.
phases.roomSummary: The room should feel immediately that this is not a prompting hackathon. It is a day of steering agents, building with them in a real repo, and later testing what holds up without us.
facilitatorPrompts: We are not starting with a tool demo or a prompting contest. We are starting a day of steering work with coding agents so something durable stays behind.
You will learn, build, reshuffle, and continue from a fresh read. That day arc is the point, not workshop logistics.
We are not learning to prompt better. We are learning to build a repo and workflow where the agent and a fresh reader can continue safely.
Whatever stays only in table talk will not carry into the afternoon.
watchFors: The opening slips into rules before it offers ambition and a reason to care.
The room reads the day as a prompting contest or a feature race.
The facilitator explains the continuation mechanic too early instead of simply setting the bar for continuation.
checkpointQuestions: Where would a fresh reader find this without you?
What is actually verified here?
What should the room carry into Build Phase 1?

facilitatorRunner.goal: Open the day as a shared room start, not as an operating brief.
say: We are not starting with a tool demo or a prompting contest.
You will learn, build, reshuffle, and continue from a fresh read. That arc is the point of the day.
We are not learning to prompt better. We are learning to build a repo and workflow where the agent and a fresh reader can continue safely.
show: Run the launch arc from framing through analogy and room activation into the first working contract.
Show the participant surface only briefly at the end of opening or in the talk bridge.
do: Do a fast room grouping by current experience with AI agents and let two short voices land.
Keep the launch brisk: each beat should change the room state, not explain the whole workshop.
watch: The opening must not turn into an internal operating memo.
The room should feel the ambition of the day before it gets the first rule.
fallback: If time slips, keep the framing, the analogy, and the first contract. Shorten the activation to a quick show of hands.
If energy drops, return to the line that the afternoon will test what the work can carry forward without authors in the room.

scenes.label: Framing
scenes.title: Today you learn to shape the work so anyone — or any agent — can carry it
scenes.body: That's the craft underneath all the agent hype. Not better prompts. Not faster typing. A repo, a workflow, a context that carries the next move without you standing over it. By tonight you'll have built one with your hands, watched fresh collaborators step into it, and seen how it holds up. The next day you open a coding agent, you'll know what to do differently.
facilitatorNotes: Open with ambition and the participant destination, not logistics.
The hero title is the day's spine — read it out loud, then pause.
Cut any reference to prompt theatre or contest framing.

blocks.title: Today you learn to shape the work so anyone — or any agent — can carry it
blocks.body: That's the craft underneath all the agent hype. Not better prompts. Not faster typing. A repo, a workflow, a context that carries the next move without you standing over it. By tonight you'll have built one with your hands, watched fresh readers step into it, and seen how it holds up. The next day you open a coding agent, you'll know what to do differently.

blocks.title: The main line for today
blocks.body: We are not learning to prompt better. We are learning to build a working system that carries the next move — whether the next reader is another participant who joined today or a fresh agent you open the next day.

blocks.title: What should change today
blocks.body: The next morning you open a fresh agent in your own repo. That agent arrives without your memory. What you practice today is how you treat every session — not once, for every session.

scenes.label: Agenda
scenes.title: Today at a glance.
scenes.body: What happens when: opening, build, rotation, and reveal.
facilitatorNotes: Give the room the times and the shape of the day.
Keep it concrete. No rules yet, just the agenda.

blocks.title: Learn, build, reshuffle, continue
blocks.body: Four beats shape the whole day. Talk sets the spine. Demo shows the first ten minutes of harness engineering with a coding agent. Build puts it in your hands. In the afternoon the work gets picked up by another participant or a fresh agent who wasn't there when it started — the test of what you built.

items.title: Talk · 10:00
items.body: Tight framing: what harness engineering is and why it matters now.

items.title: Build 1 · 10:45
items.body: Pick a brief and ship a first working version before lunch.

items.title: Reshuffle · 13:30
items.body: Work gets handed to a fresh reader. Your repo is read and continued without you explaining it.

items.title: Continue · 14:15
items.body: The second push extends the work under fresh eyes. The reveal is what holds up.

scenes.label: Find your seat (room)
scenes.title: Line up by experience. Find a seat. Introduce yourself.
scenes.body: Wall scene that holds for the full nine minutes of orientation.
facilitatorNotes: Hold this on the wall for the full nine minutes of orientation.
After the count-off, let participants find a seat and introduce themselves briefly to the person next to them.
Advance only once everyone is seated.

blocks.title: Line up by experience. Find a seat. Introduce yourself.
blocks.body: Everyone stands. Line up by experience along the wall. Count off and find a seat. Sit down and do quick introductions with the person next to you.

blocks.title: How the next nine minutes go
items: 1 · Line up by experience (2 min)
2 · Count off and find a seat (1 min)
3 · Short introductions at your table (5 min)
4 · Look up when the room is ready (1 min)

scenes.label: Handoff to the talk
scenes.title: Seats are set. Look up.
scenes.body: Brief transition beat. Facilitator moves the room into the talk.
facilitatorNotes: Short bridge between orientation and the talk.
May be collapsed into a verbal transition if orientation runs long.

blocks.title: Seats are set. Look up.
blocks.body: Everyone is seated. The room is moving into the talk.

participantMoments.label: Look up and orient
participantMoments.title: Stay with the room
participantMoments.body: No action yet. Listen for the shape of the day and the bar the work must hold up to.

blocks.title: Stay with the room
blocks.body: No action yet. Listen for the day arc and the standard for work that holds up under a fresh read.

participantMoments.label: Look up
participantMoments.title: Seats are set. Look up.
participantMoments.body: The room is moving into the talk. Pause the table setup and switch your attention back to the front.

blocks.title: Seats are set. Look up.
blocks.body: Everyone is seated. The room is moving into the talk.

phases.label: The Craft Underneath
phases.goal: Turn the opening energy into a precise thesis: harness engineering is the infrastructure for working with agents, and the first build move must begin with map, boundaries, and proof rather than another prompt.
phases.roomSummary: By the end of the talk, the room should see AGENTS.md, skills, runbooks, and explicit verification as working infrastructure rather than optional polish, and Build Phase 1 should feel like the obvious next move.
facilitatorPrompts: We are not learning to prompt better. We are learning to build a repo and workflow where the agent and a fresh reader can continue safely.
Everyone tried prompting. The question is whether the next reader can continue without you.
Harness engineering is what holds up when the room is gone.
After this talk, the room should return to the repo with a concrete operating system, not with a search for a smarter prompt.
watchFors: The thesis gets lost in long examples.
The room hears prompting tips instead of workflow discipline.
The facilitator rushes the bridge into build and the precision is lost.
checkpointQuestions: What will you move from your head or from chat into the repo today?
Which repo signal would help a fresh reader continue?
Which explicit check will be your trust boundary today?

facilitatorRunner.goal: Land a precise thesis the room can build against.
say: Harness engineering is the infrastructure around every coding-agent session.
You need context, boundaries, and checks that hold up as the model changes.
show: Walk the four pillars: goal, context, constraints, done-when.
Use the lead analogy sparingly — it is a bridge, not the thesis.
do: Keep the talk tight. Every minute should change the room's mental model.
End with a clear hand-off into the demo.
watch: The talk should not drift into generic agent hype.
Watch for the room shifting into prompting examples.
fallback: If time slips, keep the thesis, the four pillars, and the bridge.
If attention drops, keep the core line and one concrete build expectation.

scenes.label: Something changed
scenes.title: While we argued about prompts, something changed
scenes.body: Six months ago the conversation was still about prompts. Today the serious voices are talking about the system around the agent — what it reads, what bounds it, what proves the work.
facilitatorNotes: Evidence scene only. Deliberate pause after each named voice.
If time is tight, cut to Lopopolo, Böckeler, and Stripe.

blocks.title: While we argued about prompts, something changed
blocks.body: The same pattern showed up fast: Lopopolo shipping at scale, Böckeler naming harness engineering, Guo naming the role shift, Stripe proving it in production.

scenes.label: Why this matters now
scenes.title: Why this matters now
scenes.body: No one can tell you what agentic coding looks like by the end of 2026. That uncertainty is exactly why the craft matters now.
facilitatorNotes: Keep this scene short. It is urgency, not another evidence parade.

blocks.title: The work has to hold up to the next model
blocks.body: You do not need to know which model wins next. You need context, boundaries, and checks that hold up as the next model arrives.

blocks.title: What stays, what changes
blocks.body: The model changes. The harness holds. That's the bet this day is built on.

scenes.label: The four pillars
scenes.title: How you actually build one
scenes.body: Fowler splits it into guides and sensors. OpenAI talks about context, constraints, and feedback. Here is the four-pillar version you can actually build with before lunch.
facilitatorNotes: Pillars scene only. The role-shift teaching now lives in the next scene.
If time is tight, collapse guides and sensors into one sentence.

blocks.title: How you actually build one
blocks.body: Four pillars. Four moves. All visible in the repo.

items.title: Context as infrastructure.
items.body: AGENTS.md, skills, runbooks. If it isn't in a file, the agent cannot rely on it.

items.title: Guides — steering before it acts.
items.body: Rules, templates, and constraints that steer before the agent acts.

items.title: Sensors — catching after it acts.
items.body: Tracer bullets, end-to-end checks, and review that catch drift after the agent acts.

items.title: Managing, not chatting.
items.body: Set direction, scope, and verification. Do not type every move live.

scenes.label: Managing, not chatting
scenes.title: Managing agents changes your role
scenes.body: The tool is not the shift. Your role is.
facilitatorNotes: Role-shift scene. Keep it crisp and human, not managerial jargon.

blocks.title: You're not writing code alongside the agent. You're shaping the system it runs in.
blocks.body: The craft shifts. You build the context, the boundaries, the checks. The agent moves inside them.

blocks.quote: A good lead builds the system the work runs in.

blocks.title: A lead builds the system the work runs in.
blocks.body: Same frame for agents. Your job is the harness around them.

scenes.label: You've been sitting inside one
scenes.title: You've been sitting inside one all morning
scenes.body: The workshop skill. The dashboard. The browser participant surface. The repos you'll work in today. I built all of it with agents, using the discipline we just named. Not as a sales pitch — as proof. The craft is real. It works. Today we're practicing inside an example of it.
facilitatorNotes: Facilitator transition line: "Let me show you what I mean — here'  what a working agent session actually looks like when the harness is doing its job."

blocks.title: You've been sitting inside one all morning
blocks.body: The workshop skill, the dashboard, the browser participant surface, and the repos you'll work in today — all of it was built with agents, using the discipline we just named. What you're about to see in the demo is a working instance, not a promise.

participantMoments.label: Stay with the room
participantMoments.title: Listen for the shift
participantMoments.body: No table action yet. Track the contrast: prompts are not the system; the repo contract around the agent is.

blocks.title: Listen for the shift
blocks.body: No table action yet. Stay with the argument and the contrast.

participantMoments.label: Name one gap
participantMoments.title: Find the weakest signal in your repo
participantMoments.body: Pick the one gap you already suspect. Don't solve it yet. Name it cleanly so Build 1 starts from something real.

blocks.title: The gap you already suspect
blocks.body: Pick the one gap you already suspect. Name it cleanly before Build 1 starts.

participantMoments.label: Get ready for Build 1
participantMoments.title: Return to the repo with one map, one boundary, one proof target
participantMoments.body: You are about to go back to the repo. Carry one explicit next move: what will map the work, what will bound it, and what will prove it.

blocks.title: Return to the repo with one map, one boundary, one proof target
blocks.body: Build 1 should start from one explicit operating contract, not from more prompt exploration.

phases.label: Let me show you
phases.goal: Use one compact story to show a real working line: context, bounded next steps, implementation, change control, and explicit fallback moves when something goes wrong.
phases.roomSummary: The room should see quality rise as context, bounded next steps, and change control enter the flow. The point is not magic output, model mystique, or a feature parade.
facilitatorPrompts: One story-driven example, not a feature list.
The tool alone is not enough. The working system around it is what matters.
Review is not an emergency brake at the end. It is part of the workflow.
watchFors: The demo runs long and waits on generation.
It shows five modes of work instead of one clear line.
There are no explicit closure points after AGENTS.md, /plan, and /review.
checkpointQuestions: What in this flow carries intent and constraints?
Where can you see that change control is part of the process?
What from this do you need to be able to build in your own repo?

facilitatorRunner.goal: Use one compact story to show a real working line: context, bounded next steps, implementation, change control, and explicit fallback moves when something goes wrong.
say: One story-driven example, not a feature list.
The tool alone is not enough. The working system around it is what matters.
Review is not an emergency brake at the end. It is part of the workflow.
watch: The demo runs long and waits on generation.
It shows five modes of work instead of one clear line.
There are no explicit closure points after AGENTS.md, /plan, and /review.

scenes.label: Same prompt, two repos
scenes.title: Same prompt. Two repos. Watch what happens.
scenes.body: I'm going to run the same simple prompt in two repos — one bare, one with a harness. Same words. Same model. Watch what changes.
facilitatorNotes: Live demo. Pre-prepared screenshots of both Folder A drift and Folder B aligned output must be ready on a second screen.
If live generation exceeds ~90 seconds, switch to screenshots mid-demo.

sourceRefs.label: Demo script: Let me show you

blocks.title: Same brief, two folders, very different continuations
blocks.body: Folder A runs against the prompt alone. Folder B runs against the same brief plus AGENTS.md, a short plan, and the prepared workshop support path.

blocks.title: What each folder has
items: Folder A: prompt only. No repo context, no plan.
Folder B: same brief. Plus AGENTS.md (Goal, Context, Constraints, Done When). Plus a short plan. Plus a workshop support path ready when you want it.

scenes.label: The full flow
scenes.title: Now watch it hold together
scenes.body: I'm staying in Folder B. I'm going to run the actual workflow — the one you'll be running in twenty minutes. Four beats. Watch how each one feeds the next.
facilitatorNotes: Keep the beats crisp. One pass, four moves.

sourceRefs.label: Demo script: Let me show you

blocks.title: Four artifacts keep the harness upright
blocks.body: They sit in the repo, not in anyone's head. Any fresh reader — human or agent — can pick the work up from them.

blocks.title: The four artifacts
items: Goal, Context, Constraints, Done When. Four headings. The agent reads this first, every session. This is the shared working memory.
A short plan, updated as the work moves. Not a novel — just enough for continuation.
A tight working flow: write, commit, review, repeat. Small steps, clear checks.
Review is not a panic brake at the end. It's how the agent and the next reader agree on what just shipped.

blocks.title: The review is part of the harness
blocks.body: Not a separate step after everything is done. It's how the work stays legible as it moves.

scenes.label: Your toolkit
scenes.title: Your toolkit, same discipline
scenes.body: You've been watching me work in Folder B. In a moment, you'll be working in your own repo. People will reshuffle during the day, but your in-progress work stays with you. The foundation for today is the browser participant surface. The workshop skill is an optional faster path on your own machine when your setup is ready. Let me show you that path on my machine so you see what it adds.
facilitatorNotes: Live install on stage. Pre-recorded 30-second fallback video cued and ready to play in under 10 seconds if anything breaks.
Test the install fresh on the facilitator's machine the morning of.

sourceRefs.label: Demo script: Let me show you

blocks.title: Your toolkit, same discipline
blocks.body: The browser participant surface is the stable path for everyone. The workshop skill is an optional faster path on your own machine. Watch me install it here so you see what it adds when your setup is ready.

items.title: A fast path in your own repo.
items.body: If your local setup is ready, install the skill directly where you already work. If not, keep moving from the participant surface.

items.title: The current moment without switching context.
items.body: The participant surface shows what is happening right now. The skill can pull the same thing into your terminal: today's phase, your brief, the reference material, and the next move.

items.title: Reference material when you're stuck.
items.body: Patterns, examples, checks. Not a tutorial — a reference you pull at the moment of need.

items.title: A place to leave a trail.
items.body: When you find a mistake worth fixing for next time, store the fix in a file in the repo so the next person and the next agent inherit it.

blocks.title: Hashimoto's rule, operationalized.
blocks.body: Any time you find that an agent makes a mistake, take the time to engineer a solution so it does not make that mistake again. This skill and the files around it are where you do that — in a file, in the repo, for the next person. Or the next you.

scenes.label: Your first ten minutes
scenes.title: Your first ten minutes
scenes.body: In ten minutes I want everyone at the point where the agent is actually helping you draft your plan. Here's how you get there — four moves. Don't skip any of them.
facilitatorNotes: Facilitator transition to Build 1: "Clock starts now. Build Phase 1 is open."

sourceRefs.label: Demo script: Let me show you

blocks.title: Get the agent to help you draft the plan
blocks.body: In ten minutes I want everyone at the point where the agent is actually helping draft the plan. Four moves. Don't skip any.

blocks.title: Four moves
items: 1 · Open the participant surface in your browser.
Install the workshop skill only if that is the fastest move for you. If it breaks, keep moving from the participant surface and raise a hand.
2 · Pick a brief and open the repo. Don't start writing code yet.
3 · Ask the agent to draft AGENTS.md and a short plan. Review both before you accept them.

blocks.title: Agree first, then ask the agent.
blocks.body: You have to know what you want before you can ask the agent for it. Five minutes of alignment saves an hour of drift.

blocks.title: The starting move matters
blocks.body: Don't skip AGENTS.md. It's the first thing the agent reads and the first thing a fresh reader opens.

participantMoments.label: Watch the contrast
participantMoments.title: Watch what the repo changes
participantMoments.body: Stay with the demo. The point is not the command. The point is which repo artifact changes the agent's behavior.

blocks.title: Watch what the repo changes
blocks.body: Stay with the contrast. Look for the artifact that changes the outcome.

participantMoments.label: Name your first artifact
participantMoments.title: Decide what you will create first
participantMoments.body: You are about to copy this flow. Choose the first repo artifact you must create before implementation expands.

blocks.title: Decide what you will create first
blocks.body: Pick the first artifact. AGENTS.md, a plan, or the first failing test — one of them, cleanly named, before any code moves.

participantMoments.label: Open the working materials
participantMoments.title: Decide what you will create first
participantMoments.body: You are about to copy this flow. Choose the first repo artifact you must create before implementation expands.

blocks.title: Decide what you will create first
blocks.body: Pick the first artifact. AGENTS.md, a plan, or the first failing test — one of them, cleanly named, before any code moves.

phases.label: Intermezzo 1
phases.goal: Make learning visible across the room and remind participants that workflow matters as much as the artifact itself.
phases.roomSummary: A short stop between the first push and lunch. Participants write a brief check-in, compare, and the facilitator ties one principle back to what actually happened in the room.
facilitatorPrompts: Everyone is writing a check-in. Two or three sentences each.
We're looking for what's actually true right now — not a summary for show.
watchFors: Only the loudest voice speaks and everyone else goes quiet.
Check-ins drift into polished summaries instead of honest notes.
checkpointQuestions: What did you move from your head or chat into the repo?
What are you verifying today with an executable check?
What should a fresh reader read first?

facilitatorRunner.goal: Make the first round of learning visible across the room and reconnect participants to the discipline behind the output, not only to the output itself.
say: Two or three sentences each. What's actually true right now?
We're not grading the morning. We're catching what the room already knows.
show: Read the check-ins aloud, one after another.
Pull one principle that cuts across the room's notes.
do: Start with the silent retrieval scene. 3 minutes individual writing, then 2 minutes pair discussion, then room sharing.
Move briskly into the recap; don't let the writing phase stretch.
watch: The room defaults to polished summaries.
Only the loudest voice speaks while everyone else stays passive.
fallback: If time slips, skip the pair discussion and go straight to the recap.
If energy drops, read a handful of check-ins out loud to reanimate the room.

scenes.label: Write before you speak
scenes.title: Write before you speak
scenes.body: Three minutes. Individual. No talking. Write whatever comes up — short, honest, unedited. The retrieval only works if you produce before you share.
facilitatorNotes: If anyone talks during the 3 min silent phase, redirect immediately.

blocks.title: Write before you speak
blocks.body: Three minutes. Individual. No talking. Write whatever comes up — short, honest, unedited.

blocks.title: The one question to write against.
blocks.body: What surprised you about working with the agent this morning — and what does that make you want to try differently next? Three minutes. Don't talk yet.

scenes.label: Your check-in
scenes.title: Your check-in
scenes.body: Five minutes at your table. Compare what you wrote. Then write your check-in into the participant surface. Short, honest — two or three sentences is fine. It becomes part of your record.
facilitatorNotes: Append-only check-in log carries the day — the record holds up across the full day.
Encourage short, honest entries over polished summaries.

blocks.title: Your check-in
blocks.body: Compare what you wrote. Then write your check-in. Two or three sentences.

blocks.title: How to write it
items: 1 · Compare what you wrote with the person next to you (2 min).
2 · What's actually true about your work right now? What's surprising? What do you want to try differently?
3 · Write your check-in on the participant surface, in the check-in field. It saves automatically and becomes part of your record.

scenes.label: The thread
scenes.title: The thread
scenes.body: Your check-ins are on screen. I'm going to read them out loud, one after another. Then I'll pull the thread that connects them — one principle point for the morning.
facilitatorNotes: Keep the reading brisk. One check-in at a time, no debate.
Uses the participant-trail chrome preset to render all check-in trails.

blocks.title: One principle for the morning
blocks.body: Your check-ins are on screen. I read them out loud, one after another. Then I pull the thread that connects them — one principle point for the morning.

blocks.title: How the recap goes
items: Facilitator scrolls through each recorded check-in and reads it aloud. No debate, just listening.
One thread: the shared principle across the room's notes.
What each person takes into the afternoon. Verbal, or written back into the check-in — facilitator's call.

blocks.title: The thread is what's actually there
blocks.body: Every check-in is on screen. The thread is whatever is actually there, not what the facilitator hopes is there.

phases.label: Lunch
phases.goal: Interrupt the momentum before the afternoon shift and force participants to leave behind a repo a fresh reader can actually enter.
phases.roomSummary: Before lunch, participants need to clean up context, record the next safe move, and leave the repo in a state that does not rely on their memory. Lunch is not a pause in the fresh-reader constraint.
facilitatorPrompts: Fresh readers are not the end of the day. They are a design constraint throughout the day.
Write down what a fresh reader should open first.
If the same rule was said aloud twice, it belongs in the repo.
watchFors: Participants leave for lunch without a clear next step and without cleaned-up context.
The repo ends up in a state only the author can enter after the break.
checkpointQuestions: What should a fresh reader open first?
Does the repo show what is done, what is a hypothesis, and what the next safe move is?
What still lives only in conversation and must go into the repo?

sourceRefs.label: Talk: What I want them to adopt

facilitatorRunner.goal: Stop the flow before the afternoon shift and prepare the room to leave repos readable without them.
say: Clean the context. Write the next safe move. Then go to lunch.
A repo a fresh reader can enter is the bar we're holding.
show: The fresh-reader constraint. It applies across lunch.
do: During lunch, quietly check each repo. If a repo is genuinely unusable (no AGENTS.md, no code, broken setup), help the author write the minimum. Frame it as normal coaching, not a gate.
watch: People drift into lunch without cleaning up.
Context stays in heads, not in the repo.
fallback: If time slips, the minimum is an updated plan and a cleaned-up AGENTS.md.

scenes.label: Back at return time
scenes.title: Back at [return-time]
scenes.body: Eat. Walk. Breathe. See you at [return-time] sharp.
facilitatorNotes: The [return-time] placeholder is a facilitator-settable variable. Default is the scheduled resume time from Phase 1's day schedule; facilitator can override it live on the day via the scene editor.

blocks.title: Back at [return-time]
blocks.body: Eat. Walk. Breathe. See you at [return-time] sharp.

blocks.title: In case you forgot the morning's line.
blocks.body: Humans steer. Agents execute. — Ryan Lopopolo, OpenAI Frontier & Symphony

phases.label: Intermezzo 2
phases.goal: Capture what truly helped after rotation and what is emerging as a weak point in the room’s current way of working.
phases.roomSummary: The second intermezzo should pull concrete continuation signals into the open and prepare the room for reveal and reflection without drifting into vague retrospectives.
facilitatorPrompts: What helped you continue after rotation?
What was missing or slowing you down?
Which repo signal saved you the most time?
watchFors: The intermezzo stays general and without concrete examples.
You collect opinions, not signals from the repo.
No link appears to the closing reflection.
checkpointQuestions: What helped you continue after rotation?
What was missing?
Which repo signal saved you the most time?

facilitatorRunner.goal: Make the second-push prep visible and send people back with one concrete move.
say: Two or three sentences each. What do you want to try in the second push?
We're not rewriting the morning. We're catching what you want to change before you go back in.
show: Read the check-ins aloud, one after another.
Pull the shared pattern across what the room wrote.
do: Start with the silent retrieval scene. 3 minutes individual writing, then 2 minutes pair discussion, then room sharing.
watch: Only the loudest voice speaks while everyone else stays passive.
Writing stretches and the recap gets squeezed.
fallback: If time slips, skip pair discussion and read a handful of check-ins out loud.

scenes.label: Write before you speak
scenes.title: Write before you speak
scenes.body: Three minutes. Individual. No talking. Same rule as the first intermezzo — you produce before you share. This is a pause, not an ending. Write something you'd actually want to act on in the next forty minutes.
facilitatorNotes: Same silent-retrieval discipline as Intermezzo 1.

blocks.title: Write before you speak
blocks.body: Three minutes. Individual. No talking. This is a pause, not an ending.

blocks.title: The one question to write against.
blocks.body: What surprised you about the fresh read so far — and what do you want to try differently in the second push? Three minutes. Don't talk yet.

scenes.label: Your check-in
scenes.title: Your check-in
scenes.body: Five minutes at your table. Compare what you wrote. Then add your entry on the participant surface. Short, honest — two or three sentences is fine. Another entry in the story your record is telling.
facilitatorNotes: This is not the final check-in. Another entry in the append-only log.

blocks.title: Your check-in
blocks.body: Compare what you wrote. Add another entry to your record. Two or three sentences.

blocks.title: What to write
items: 1 · Compare with the person next to you (2 min).
2 · What do you want to try in the second push?
3 · Write it on the participant surface. It's another entry in the story your record is telling.

scenes.label: Back to the work
scenes.title: Back to the work
scenes.body: Your check-ins are on screen. I'll read them, pull the thread, and send you back in with one thing to try in the second push.
facilitatorNotes: Don't let Intermezzo 2 become a mini-Reveal. Keep it brisk.

blocks.title: One thing to try in the second push
blocks.body: Your check-ins are on screen. I read them, pull the thread, and send you back in with one concrete move to try.

blocks.title: How the recap goes
items: Facilitator reads each check-in aloud. No debate.
One thread: what the room is converging on.
Send everyone back in with one concrete move to try in the second push.

blocks.title: Second push is not a rewrite
blocks.body: Carry one concrete move. Don't rework everything. The room sees you in forty minutes.

phases.label: Build Phase 2 · second push
phases.goal: Apply the intermezzo's signal. Iterate, extend, push further. Commit and make the repo readable for Reveal.
phases.roomSummary: Second push of Build 2. Everyone acts on what surfaced in the intermezzo and readies the repo for the Reveal.
facilitatorPrompts: You just paused. Now act on the signal.
Commit what holds. Make the repo readable. Decide what you'll show at Reveal.
watchFors: Participants revert to overbuilding instead of closing the slice.
Final cleanup gets skipped and the Reveal lands on a repo no one can read.
checkpointQuestions: What did the intermezzo tell you? Did you act on it?
Is the repo readable by a stranger right now?
What will you show at Reveal?

facilitatorRunner.goal: Hold the tempo from the first push. The work is the same; the test is whether each person can act on the signal from the intermezzo.
say: Close the slice. Final cleanup. Leave the repo in a state the room can read.
The second push is not a rewrite. It's one concrete move from the intermezzo.
show: The fresh-reader bar. Apply it to the final state of the repo.
do: Walk the room. Watch for participants skipping final cleanup.
Coach anyone who drifts back to recovery — the minimum is a readable repo.
watch: People overwrite the slice instead of closing it cleanly.
Final cleanup is the first thing skipped when time runs short.
fallback: If someone loses the thread, switch to the recovery scene and coach them back to the smallest proof.

scenes.label: Clock resumes
scenes.title: Second push. Forty minutes. Back to the work.
scenes.body: Apply whatever you surfaced in the intermezzo. Iterate, extend, push further. At 15:30, commit, make your repo readable, decide what you'll show at Reveal.
facilitatorNotes: 30-second beat. Keep the tempo the room already knows from Build 1 and Build 2 first push.

blocks.title: Second push. Forty minutes. Back to the work.
blocks.body: Apply whatever you surfaced in the intermezzo. Iterate, extend, push further. At 15:30, commit and decide what you'll show at Reveal.

scenes.label: The second push
scenes.title: Forty minutes left. Make it count.
scenes.body: The work is the same discipline. The test now is whether you can take the signal from the intermezzo, sharpen the same story, and land it cleanly for Reveal.
facilitatorNotes: Hold the timeline on the wall for the full stretch — don't switch back to recovery scenes unless someone asks.
The timeline is a shared map; individual coaching happens at tables, not on the wall.
If someone opens a wholly new direction, pull them back to the smallest proof worth the Reveal.

blocks.title: Forty minutes left. Make it count.
blocks.body: Same discipline as the first push. This time, use the intermezzo to sharpen the same story and land it.

items.title: 14:50–15:20 · Build the second proof.
items.body: Thirty minutes of building. Apply what the intermezzo surfaced. Extend only what the signal justifies.

items.title: 15:20–15:30 · Land the reveal slice.
items.body: Ten minutes. Commit. Make the repo readable. Pick the one thing you will show at Reveal.

blocks.title: Don't open a new front.
blocks.body: Use the intermezzo to sharpen the same story, not to start a different one. If you get lost, return to the smallest proof.

phases.label: Reveal and reflection
phases.goal: Close the day by naming the signals that helped the work carry forward after the reshuffle and fresh read, and turn them into next practice rather than a pleasant ending.
phases.roomSummary: The Reveal and reflection aren't a competition. We watch what holds the work up across the day and what grinds it down.
facilitatorPrompts: We're not grading anyone.
We're looking at what actually carried the work across the day.
watchFors: Reflection slides into comparison of who did better.
The facilitator's read drifts from the evidence into general praise.
checkpointQuestions: What helped you continue?
What was missing?
What will you do differently next week?

facilitatorRunner.goal: Close the day on evidence, not on encouragement. Reflect against the records.
say: We are not judging who did better.
We look at what carried the work across the day.
show: Point at specific check-in records as evidence.
Surface the shared patterns, not individual stories.
do: Open with 1-2-4-All. Close with a personal commitment.
Read the check-in records during the buffer, not during the Reveal itself.
watch: The reflection turns into comparing who performed better.
Facilitator's read drifts from evidence into general encouragement.
fallback: If time slips, keep the 1-2-4-All and the personal commitment. Cut the facilitator's outside read.

scenes.label: Alone, pairs, fours, all
scenes.title: Alone, pairs, fours, all
scenes.body: Four layers of reflection. Each one a little wider than the last. This is the most important twenty minutes of the day — do not rush it.
facilitatorNotes: Full 1-2-4-All. Real time on every layer (3 + 4 + 5 + 10 min).

blocks.title: What carried the work. What ground it down.
blocks.body: We look for the signals that helped the work carry fresh input, and the ones that broke under pressure. Every recurring pain across the room is a candidate for a better template, check, or runbook — not a personal failure.

items.title: 1 · Alone
items.body: One minute silent. What carried? What ground it down?

items.title: 2 · Pairs
items.body: Two minutes with the person next to you. Compare notes.

items.title: 4 · Fours
items.body: Four minutes in a small cluster. Surface the shared pattern.

items.title: All · Room
items.body: Shared harvest across the room. One thread at a time.

blocks.title: Patterns, not blame
blocks.body: Recurring pain across the room is a system signal, not a personal failure. The next version of this day fixes it in the template.

scenes.label: Show us what you built
scenes.title: Show us what you built
scenes.body: Each person gets five minutes. You just spent twenty minutes naming what you learned — now show us what it carries. Run the tracer. Walk the repo. Tell us the thing that surprised you most.
facilitatorNotes: Five minutes per participant, no Q&A during demos. Hold questions for later.
With 6+ participants, decide before Scene 10.2 between 5 and 4 minutes per person.

blocks.title: Five minutes each. What does your repo carry?
blocks.body: Run the tracer. Walk the repo. Tell us the thing that surprised you most.

blocks.title: How each demo goes
items: One person at a time, five minutes each.
No Q&A during demos. Hold questions for later.
Run the tracer. Walk the repo. Tell us the surprise.

blocks.title: The demo is the evidence
blocks.body: Not a pitch. Not a summary. The tracer and the repo do the talking.

scenes.label: What I saw today
scenes.title: What I saw today
scenes.body: Now my part. I walked the room all day. I read every check-in record as the afternoon moved. Here's what I saw from outside — the patterns, the surprises, the things the room collectively figured out that maybe nobody felt individually.
facilitatorNotes: The hardest moment of the day. Prepare by reading each check-in record during the buffer before the Reveal, not with generic closing notes.
Uses the participant-trail chrome preset so the facilitator can point to specific entries in the records as evidence.

blocks.title: What I saw
blocks.body: I walked the room all day. I read every check-in record as the afternoon moved. Here's what I saw from outside — the patterns, the surprises, the things the room collectively figured out.

blocks.title: Grounded in the evidence
blocks.body: The facilitator's observations should be grounded in what's on the check-in records and what was just shared in the 1-2-4-All and the demos. Not general encouragement. Specific signals, specific surprises, specific patterns. If you can't point at it, don't say it.

scenes.label: The one thing you'll change
scenes.title: The one thing you'll change
scenes.body: One sentence. Specific enough that you could tell, a week from now, whether you actually did it. Write it first. Say it out loud.
facilitatorNotes: Writing scene only. Storage moves to the next scene.
No Monday framing — anchored to "the next time I work with an agent."

blocks.title: The one thing you'll change
blocks.body: One sentence. Specific enough that you could tell, a week from now, whether you actually did it.

items.title: Write the sentence (2 min).
items.body: Format: "The next time I work with an agent, the first thing I'll change is [specific action] — because [specific reason from today]." No vague language. No "I will try to…"

items.title: Say it out loud at your table (3 min).
items.body: One person at a time. No debate, no defense. Just read it.

blocks.title: Specific enough to check.
blocks.body: Not: "I'll work better with agents." Yes: "The next time I start an agent task, I'll write Goal, Context, Constraints, and Done-When in AGENTS.md before typing anything into the prompt." If you can't tell a week from now whether you did it, the sentence isn't specific enough yet.

scenes.label: Save the commitment
scenes.title: Put it where you'll see it next time
scenes.body: The sentence only matters if you can find it when the next session starts.
facilitatorNotes: Storage scene. Participants pick one home and do it now.

blocks.title: What holds up tomorrow
blocks.body: Name the one commitment you want to carry into your next session. Write it down while it's still clear.

scenes.label: Go use it
scenes.title: Go use it
scenes.body: This is where my part ends and yours continues. You built something today that a stranger continued without you — that was the test, and you passed. The craft you practiced is yours. The materials stay with you.
facilitatorNotes: 30-60 seconds of screen time. The facilitator's closing words are more important than what's on the slide.
No "see you on Monday." No pep talk. The room feels more when the closing undersells.

blocks.title: Go use it
blocks.body: This is where my part ends and yours continues. You built something today that a stranger continued without you — that was the test, and you passed. The craft you practiced is yours. I won't be in the room next time you open an agent session — but everything you need is already in your hands.

blocks.title: The follow-up package.
blocks.body: The workshop skill and reference materials live with you after today. When you want to pull a pattern, a check, or a reminder, ask the skill. It knows what you learned because you were part of building it.

briefs.title: Code Review Helper
briefs.problem: Code review quality varies depending on who is looking. Some changes arrive with a sharp checklist and clear risk framing; others come through unevenly and consistency breaks exactly where it should be tightest. Your job: design a tool that turns a diff into a usable review checklist — one that says what it is certain about, what is heuristic suggestion, and what still needs human judgment.
userStories: As a reviewer, I want a fast checklist of risks, questions, and focus areas from a diff.
As the author of a change, I want to know what I should verify before the review starts.
As a fresh reader coming into this repo, I want to continue from the heuristics already discovered instead of reinventing them.
architectureNotes: This can be a CLI, web app, or simple script. The important part is a clear `diff -> analysis -> checklist` flow.
It must be obvious which inputs the tool expects and what it cannot evaluate reliably.
Add a seed diff or `examples/` so the workflow can be checked locally.
Separate heuristic from certainty clearly. The tool should help the reviewer, not pretend to be infallible.
acceptanceCriteria: A fresh reader can add a new review rule within 10 minutes without reading through the whole codebase. *(Fresh-reader test.)*
The tool produces a review checklist from a seed diff.
The output clearly separates certain findings from heuristic suggestions from items still needing human judgment.
The review rules themselves are readable and editable by someone who didn't write them.
There is at least one concrete example in `examples/` that demonstrates the full flow.
briefs.firstAgentPrompt: Don't start with code. First write the review rules, define what a good checklist looks like for a specific seed diff, and clarify what the tool *cannot* evaluate reliably. Only then propose the first implementation slice.

briefs.title: DevToolbox CLI
briefs.problem: Every codebase accumulates small one-off scripts — log cleaners, JSON parsers, commit lookups — that work for one person until nobody remembers how to run them. Your job: design a CLI that solves a few real developer pain points and stays legible after a fresh read. Not a bag of scripts. A small system where the next command, test, and doc have an obvious home.
userStories: As a developer, I want to turn a log or JSON blob into a readable format with one command.
As a developer, I want to quickly find suspicious commits, branches, or changes without manually assembling git commands.
As a working group, we want both the commands and the way of working documented so a fresh collaborator can continue without confusion.
architectureNotes: Choose any language or framework, but the CLI must stay easy to run and easy to discover.
Separate commands from helper utilities and configuration from the start.
`AGENTS.md` should describe the build and test flow, output conventions, and the rules for future extension.
A runbook for someone joining the repo after lunch matters as much as a working command.
Do not build a bag of scripts. Build a small system where it is obvious where the next command, test, and doc belong.
acceptanceCriteria: A fresh collaborator can add or fix a command within 10 minutes of opening the repo. *(Fresh-reader test.)*
At least 3 working commands, each solving a concrete developer pain point.
`README` and `AGENTS.md` explain how to run and verify locally.
The extension pattern is visible — a new command fits without breaking the structure.
Every command has at least one readable input/output example.
briefs.firstAgentPrompt: Don't start with code. Start with `AGENTS.md`, a short plan for the extension pattern, and one clear verification step. Only then implement the first command.

briefs.title: Doc Generator
briefs.problem: Documentation goes stale almost immediately. Once maintenance is fully manual, it gets postponed, and after a few iterations nobody knows whether the docs still describe reality. After a fresh read, a fresh reader no longer knows what to trust and what is only inference. Your job: design a tool that generates baseline technical documentation — or a structured project overview — from an existing codebase in a way that makes *what we know* and *what we're inferring* visibly different.
userStories: As a developer, I want basic technical documentation from a project without writing everything from scratch.
As a reviewer, I want to understand the module structure and main entry points within minutes.
As a fresh collaborator, I want to discover the architecture without long detective work.
architectureNotes: The input can be a local repo, seed directory, or simplified dataset.
The output can be Markdown, HTML, or a simple text report.
The important part is to explain what the tool can infer reliably and what remains heuristic.
Design the structure from the start so another output type can be added later.
Do not optimize for AI theatre. Optimize for trust, traceability, and a clear next safe move for a fresh collaborator.
acceptanceCriteria: A reviewer can read the generated documentation and tell exactly where each claim came from — within minutes, without opening the original source. *(Fresh-reader test — provenance is the whole point.)*
A fresh collaborator can add a new output format (Markdown → HTML, report → overview) without refactoring the whole tool.
The generated output makes it obvious which claims are *certain* (read directly from source) and which are *inference* (heuristic).
The tool produces at least one complete, readable output for a real or seed codebase.
`README` and `AGENTS.md` explain how to run locally and what input the tool expects.
briefs.firstAgentPrompt: Start by listing every signal the tool will read from a codebase (file names, imports, commit messages, directory structure, comments — whatever you're choosing) and for each one, decide whether it produces *certain* output or *heuristic* output. Write that spec in `AGENTS.md`. Only then propose the first implementation slice.

briefs.title: Metrics Dashboard
briefs.problem: Every codebase has the data — user signups, deploy frequency, error rates, feature usage — scattered across different tools. The shared screen that turns it into a decision-making view usually doesn't exist. Your job: design a dashboard that puts a few metrics and one trend on one screen, stays cleanly separated between data and UI, and is still legible to a fresh reader who didn't build it.
userStories: As a user, I want several metrics on one screen so the current state is legible quickly.
As a facilitator, I want to change seed data without touching UI logic.
As a fresh collaborator, I want to understand the data structure, components, and screens within minutes.
architectureNotes: Separate seed data from UI from the first commit.
Mobile-first is an advantage, but the dashboard must remain readable on a projected screen too.
README and monitoring should explain what already works, what is still mock, and what is still missing.
Design the structure so adding another metric does not require rewriting the whole screen.
Do not optimize only for the look. Make the data model, layout rules, and verification path easy for a fresh collaborator to understand.
acceptanceCriteria: A fresh collaborator can add a new metric within 10 minutes without breaking the layout. *(Fresh-reader test.)*
The dashboard shows at least 3 metrics and one trend or comparison.
Seed data and UI logic are cleanly separated — a facilitator can swap seed data without touching the UI.
The layout stays legible on mobile and on a large projected screen; the `README` says how to test both.
The `README` documents which parts are real, which are mocked, and which are still missing.
briefs.firstAgentPrompt: Don't start with the UI. Start with the data model, the component boundaries, the layout rules, and a `Done When` in `AGENTS.md`. Only then touch any visual code.

briefs.title: Standup Bot
briefs.problem: Daily standups in chat become long threads where blockers get lost, dependencies between people are invisible, and nothing is reconstructable an hour later. Your job: design a tool that turns standup inputs into an overview a fresh collaborator can continue working from — without the original author, without the original thread, and without a verbal walkthrough.
userStories: As a lead, I want standup responses collected into one readable summary.
As a developer, I want to quickly see blockers, dependencies, and topics that need coordination.
As a fresh collaborator, I want to understand the data flow and integration points without a verbal walkthrough.
architectureNotes: Prefer a clear data model over complicated integration.
Mock data is fine if the workflow feels realistic and is documented clearly.
Separate ingest, processing, and presentation of the output.
Prompts, runbooks, and decisions must live in the repo, not only in the heads of the original authors.
Do not optimize for pretty summary prose before blockers, dependencies, and the next safe move are visible.
acceptanceCriteria: A fresh collaborator can continue this project without a verbal explanation. *(Fresh-reader test.)*
The tool ingests seed data in a documented format and produces an overview that surfaces blockers, dependencies, and items needing coordination.
The output distinguishes what the tool is certain about from what is only a heuristic suggestion.
The repo explains how to connect the ingest to a real input channel — without requiring it.
Ingest, processing, and presentation are cleanly separated in the code.
briefs.firstAgentPrompt: Don't start with code. Start with: the seed data format, the output data model, the rules that distinguish certainty from heuristic, and an `AGENTS.md` a fresh reader will open first. Only then propose the first implementation slice.

challenges.title: Create AGENTS.md as a map
challenges.description: Write down the goal, build and test flow, durable rules, and where a fresh reader should look first.

challenges.title: Write a code review skill
challenges.description: At least one repeatable review routine must live in the repo as a skill or runbook, not only in a prompt.

challenges.title: Use /plan before coding
challenges.description: Let the agent plan the work first, show what the plan was based on, and record the next safe move.

challenges.title: Add the smallest useful verification
challenges.description: Create a RED test, tracer bullet, or simple browser check before you give the agent more autonomy.

challenges.title: Run 2 parallel Codex sessions
challenges.description: Split the problem into two independent streams and compare what actually worked.

challenges.title: Add 'Done When' to every task
challenges.description: Every important task needs an explicit completion criterion and a link to verification.
